So, kind of without further ado, you got Speaker, Writer, Business Guru, Business Coach, a legend, Dan Kennedy. Oh, thank you very much. Not last to start. Thanks Jack. You will see, contrary to the introduction, you will see less of a platform presentation than sort of a discussion. The, let's see. Give me a hand, Jack, will you? We're going to bring this as far to the right as you can. Okay, otherwise I'm going to be walking back in front of the way you have the projector rigged. So a couple things, I know a couple of you guys. Pete. Richard. Those of you who are docs, I do a lot of work in that field. I just spent a day with a team with five dermatology practices in New York, about a $30 million a year business, and they are opening acne treatment standalone centers. You think Aspen, Denil, if you want a frame of reference. Yesterday on my way down here, I was in Richmond at Dr. Martin's practice. If you're in Denil, you might know Charlie. Last year, average case value $42,600, and a 38% year-to-year growth, with double digit year-to-year growth every year in the past five years. The number one Invisalign doc in the country, Dr. Donna Galante is also whatever you want to call it, student, follower, you know, whatever. I happen to have a client in Bahrain who comes and visits me once or twice every year. Flowing robes, bald head, the whole works. He informed me early that I needed to do a good job because basically he was, by his religion, sworn to kill me if he couldn't convert me. So we have a real good understanding. All the sharp objects are put away before he arrives. I always ask him how his flight was, and of course he says terrible. He was strip searched and I say that's the best piece of news I've had. Albert, so if you were at that hurricane thing, as he said early 80s, and those seminars, I did eight or nine with Gary, those were $8,000 per person, three-day extravagances, and that's in the 80s, $8,000 price tag, pretty good price tag. Sometimes as many as 300 people there, and basically one hot seat, if you know what a hot seat is, after another, after another, after another. The baker guy, very good friend of mine is on the board of Great Harvest Bread Company, and branded foods. I have a couple of clients who have been on the food network a number of times. I have a client who is on QVC all the time. So many of us have something in common, but they asked me very specifically today to talk about this whole sort of step above celebrity, step above authority, step above the works, to living legend status. He mentioned the success tour, and I'm not sure everybody has that frame of reference, but it's now called Get Motivated. It's still around, mostly sports arenas. Our biggest audience in the nine years that I did every single one of them was over 37,000 people, and we had a lot of 10,000, and 12,000, and 18,000 person days. And so early on in that environment, so those days, by the time we was over with, there would be, I don't know, 20 speakers during the day. And it was this long day, monkey with anything you want to monkey with, okay? Nothing you could possibly do, except leave that down there when you're done. Please, too. I'm going to set this over just a little bit. Keep going on. Don't mind me. I got it. So Zig and I, so that thing started with its owner, Peter Lowe, Zig and me, and half days. And then added a memory guy, and a big breakthrough was Reagan, by the way, and I want to come back to that. It changed everything. And what you really, one of the best things you could walk out of your however many days you are here for with is one thing, not a hundred things, and certainly not a thousand things. There's no price for how many pages of notes you take. But one big thing that could change everything. And for Peter, that was Reagan, and I'll come back to that. So Zig and I are backstage in this crappy little civic theater in Youngstown, Ohio. And if you don't know Youngstown, Ohio, it's sort of the boarded up business poverty capital of the Midwest. It hasn't thrived since the steel industry went away and the mafia went away. So Youngstown's sort of this nothing place. And we're backstage in the green room, which is really just a corner of a dark area with a curtain around it in the morning before this thing starts. About 500 people out there. And the guy who is hosting, who is, I don't know his title, but he's in charge of the Youngstown Civic Theater. He is like a big fan of Zig, which makes sense. He also was a big fan of me, which made less sense, particularly 20-some-odd years ago. But he is like, orgasmically excited that we are there. And so he is, I'm sure you've had these people around you. I call them Velcro guys. He has no sense of physical space. And he is like Velcroed to the two of us. You move, he moves, you back up, he moves with you. It's like you set the Fred Astaire dance thing. There's a reference for you. And so he is just climaxing over and over and over and over and over again at how excited he is. And in comes this volunteer young woman whose job it is to refill the coffee and the tea and swat the flies off the croissants. And so she comes in and she's, I guess, her to be 17 tops, tiny little thing, razor blade hanging from one ear, couple of tats, and a thoroughly disgusted, annoyed look on her face. This clearly was not her choice of things to do today. And he, like, vibrating with his excitement. He grabs her and spins her around to face us. And he can barely put words together. He's so excited. He's, you got to be, these are like the most famous people we've ever had. And she looks at Zeg and she starts at the tip of his head and kind of all the way down to her shoes and all the way back up. And she does the same thing with me, all the way down to the shoes and all the way back up. And she says, they don't look famous to me. So what? So there's fame and there's fame and there's fame and there's fame. You know, mine is not the kind that we have to worry about paparazzi on the lunch break or you getting accosted in the park or if you're with me or at an airport. It is more purposed, which is one of the many things that I do want to talk about today is the difference between fame for ego, fame for fun, fame just to be famous, which can sometimes turn into money, but usually for a relatively short period of time or really purposed fame for people who are in business. I do have one other quick story to tell you. We got a lot of work to do. I shouldn't even be doing it, but it's fun. So you all know who Larry King is, yes? Okay. So Larry was on with us quite a bit. And after an event in San Francisco, Larry and I are at the airport and I was flying commercial then, I don't anymore and so, and he was flying commercial that day for some reason, which he usually didn't do. But so we are at the two furthest gates you can be at and we're actually there early for our flights. And so we're sitting together talking. We're like the only ones there and now pretty soon people start to arrive. And when you do one of these events in California, there's a lot of population density and it's nothing to fly from LA to San Fran or back and forth. Southwest has a flight every 10-clump of them. And as they arrive, Larry gets up out of his chair and he kind of steps between me and them and says, hi, I'm Larry King. This is my one tip for you. If you don't get any other value, if you come to Florida, buy one of these. This is a Disney rain pouch. They sell them at every place in the park. It's about a million dollars a day in revenue every time it rains, which in case the Floridians have lied to you, that's every damn day. So you want one of these, they fold real flat, they fit, you can roll it up, put it in your pocket, but you want one of these. So if you don't get anything else out of it today, that's an important piece of advice is to have a Disney rain pouch, which to their credit, any other outdoor amusement park would view rain as a bad thing. They do not. They have these in huge stacks hidden away in every gift shop, as soon as it rains, the accountants all are doing a happy dance and everybody's getting them things out and piling them as high as they can pile them. So you have some notes in front of you, some of which we're not even going to talk about and some of which we are. So there's stuff for you to take home and use at your own discretion and some we're going to move real quick through and some we're going to spend some time on. But there's a couple of things that it's always useful to do. One, if you hear somebody like Nick or Jack or even any of you or I mentioned a name you don't know and we don't take time to, for example, explain to you who Gary Halbert was, there's this thing called Google and there's this thing called Wikipedia and there's this thing called Amazon. And this there, by the way, this is a part of the future I don't like. This is like the disembodied head in a jar. So anyway, it's worth jotting down the name and at your leisure do a little search activity to see what it is you might find. If we make a reference, let's say I mentioned carbon paper and you have no idea what carbon paper is. You might actually. Everybody comes to me. This is a rare exception. It happens to be combined with something I have to do once a year for GKIC for all our competition winners and a family trip and so but otherwise if they if we all want to get together you'd be in Cleveland and it's zero degrees by the way and I wouldn't care. So so they the Secretary of Commerce from Oklahoma and his team is there and I have a typewriter which if you can Google that and it's this thing without all its problems and I have it. I don't really use it but I have it sitting on a bookshelf and two of the young women and she bursts out that I've only seen those in old movies you know and they took their picture with it. They they did not take their picture with me by the way they took their picture with the typewriter because they thought that was you know really cool. So I think so the second thing is useful to do one is use your search engines. The other thing it's useful to do is to use a dictionary and a the czarist and as a copywriter I live in my the czarist and often smarter people than us have done a bunch of work to explain things in a way that can be useful. So just sort of as a as a basis here and I'm on your page too if you are following along at home some key words here are underlegitari are remarkable meaning worthy of being remarked upon. There's a country western song let's give him something to talk about if you aren't constantly giving your market something that is worthy of being remarked upon about you then you are very unlikely to achieve and sustain legendary status for all the reasons that you might want to do. So that's a good word. Fabled is a good word because most legends are have factual basis but grandiose exaggeration and so there's a romancing to the stone that always goes on. Ideally it gradually takes on a life of its own but you generally provide the fuel. Almost all legends you mentioned you're living in a cardboard box under the bridge with four children to feed and no money whatever your version of that story is and now Maserati's and hot and cold running guys and you know whatever the punch line of the story. So people generally are interested more in heroic figures than they are in anything else. So the fact that you bake great bread is a far less interest to anybody than that you had to start the bakery in a cardboard box under the bridge or you provide jobs for ex-convicts like the rabbi, baker, monk guy his name now escapes me but you probably would know him he's a legend. So heroic is a really good word. If we can't figure out a way to make ourselves heroic in the eyes of our marketplace again we're going to have trouble. Story is a very good word because as Nick will tell you people are more interested in human interest stories than they are in anything other than themselves and so the more we communicate by story and the less we communicate by facts figures information the more remarkable we tend to be. People will tend to retell a story more than they will retell facts and figures. Speakers have a thing called their signature story some have more than one but generally every speaker has one story above all other stories that they literally tell every single time they speak no matter what and it is a retellable story it is a story that gets told a lot and if they have a lot of longevity people actually ask for it to be told like they might ask a comedian to do a particular riff or a musician you know you would go see somebody from your youth who is now you know they have to show us to end at nine o'clock at night because it is past their bedtime and they are up there with walkers and stuff and everybody is yelling out a song that they want them to do. So story is an important word myth is a real important word because again these things tend to be embellished narrative is an important word. This legend has a linear narrative that runs through it that everything else is connected to and it is important to sort of be clear about that and continually reference it and last a set of beliefs is a very important word. This is often undervalued but people want to know what you are about what you philosophically believe if they are going to attach themselves to you and if they are going to give you money for goods or services actually more so than they want to know all the stuff everybody always wants to tell them about their products and their services. So you will hear me say it probably more than once today but so almost everybody you will ever compete with in whatever it is that you do approaches communicating with their prospects their patients their customers their clients their marketplace the media all in the same little box and they talk about products and services features and benefits what the stuff is and why it is better than everybody else's stuff products and services features and benefits price and discounts that is what everybody talks about and really hardly anybody gives a rat's patootie about that at least until they have bought in psycho emotionally to the person behind it and want to be attached to that person. So changing the whole direction that you come at the marketplace from is really what all this is about. I have just a list of legends if you group them there is a group of sort of political world leader kind of legends there is a group of business legends the last one the initials may or may not mean anything to anybody here because it is a somewhat now faded legend and I am not going to take time to tell you who it is but I told you how to find out if you are so inclined there is a group of the fictional legends that by the way many people so last time I was in London we went to the Sherlock Holmes Museum which understand on its surface is ridiculous because Sherlock Holmes is a fictional character but they have a Sherlock Holmes Museum which is an excuse for a gift shop but I mean it is a three story museum and they have a recreation of his study with all the artifacts in it which of course are not artifacts because he is a fictional individual. This does not dissuade them managing thousands of letters a year from all over the world arriving at 221 B Baker Street asking for help with everything from a lost dog to a much more serious crime addressed to this fictional individual that huge numbers of people do not differentiate is actually being a fictional individual. There are what I would call the philosophy box and so I have put a couple there and there is the business box and again legends are known so David Ogilvy is a legend in the world of advertising so those of us who live in advertising which is basically where I live he is a legendary figure. To people who do not pay attention to advertising and do not have anything to do with advertising you are going to have to go Google them if you are so inclined to figure out who the hell I am talking about. And that is so there is also a whole lot of people I do not know if you are there is a show on Fox in the afternoon it is not a very good show but it is called the five and so how many of you have seen the five so they have their token liberal they have one liberal to beat up on with all the other conservatives and he is I like him a lot he is very fine his name is Bob Beckel and he is a political operative who I doubt has ever managed a successful campaign he managed Mondale's campaign for example which some of you will have to Google Mondale. And Beckel is like he is more out of pop culture than I am and I am out somewhat so like I do not know like anybody at the Grammys it is like but Beckel like knows nobody they have to explain to him who the Kardashians are he just is completely divorced from that segment of our world so they are legendary to people who watch E and who pay attention to pop culture but not necessarily legendary to everybody has it is with everybody on this list but everybody on this list has profound commonalities which is what we are going to get to as quickly as I can and where we really want to spend all of the bulk of our time. But first if you look at page six very quickly I think it is always useful to know sort of why you set out to do something if in fact that is why you came here by the way Pete and Richard you guys knew I was going to be here well then it is gratifying that you are here. So one reason is longevity an issue of do you want to be around for a long period of time doing what you do. There are a lot of people who are around briefly and in many cases make a very big splash but then are gone and quickly forgotten. So last night which you guys were probably doing something more interesting but last night was the last Jay Leno tonight show. He has been the keeper of the keys of the show with a brief interruption for the Conan experiment for 22 years. Twenty years if you want to take that out second in tenure only to Carson. So for those of you who don't know before Carson there was Parr and there was Steve Allen. And staying there for 20 years that kind of staying power is relatively rare in the entertainment industry and it is relatively rare pretty much in every other industry that there is. So if you look at the if you take the New York Times book bestseller list and you pick the category you're in so you pick health or you pick finance or you pick motivation and personal development you pick whatever you want and you go take a look at 20 years or longer but at least take 20 years of that list and you look at it every week for those 20 years which you can do that online by the way. You will find a mountain of books and authors that were there for a week or two or three or four or five and have never been seen since. People that have staying power do things differently than those who have splash fame and fortune. And so one reason to take the kind of approach we're talking about today is if longevity interests you. My own business my own interests have been far better served by being basically a low grade infection that never leaves the body of my targeted clientele rather than being something that is splashed all over magazine covers and gets to be on Oprah but then three years later who that who what. And so that's a choice to make. Consumption is preemptive power and influence by preemptive I mean that you reach a point that it is there on its own through a combination of everything from you use the word marketing funnel serve from the sort of micro stuff we do in business to this macro stuff we reach a point that the power and influence is preemptive. So by that I mean for example the Secretary of Commerce of the state of Oklahoma sends in their facts as they are required to do at my office and indicates that they want a just at least to start with a consulting day and they have a team of six people that need to be in this consulting day and they're the question is never asked since there's seven of us and one of you can we get you to come to Oklahoma. I don't have to deal with that question because I have preemptive power and influence within this case that person who became the Secretary of Commerce of Oklahoma. And so if you want preemptive power and influence then this legendary stuff is useful talked about equal sought. So this is really a formula that helps us have to do less marketing. Now I'm an advertising and marketing guy but I don't like doing it for myself never did and the less of it I do the happier that I am. And so I call it non-marketing marketing the more people you have talking about you if they are all talking about you into the same universe then the less you have to do of anything else but keep them talking about you. And that beats having to run ads and run TV shows and hire publicists and all of the other stuff that everybody does this beats it because not only don't you have to do it but and you don't have to you can spend less money doing this than almost anything else and get better results but it also brings you a better clientele. So in January the three the two that sought me one Secretary of State of Commerce of Oklahoma why well because he's part of our GKIC world and has been part of that as a part of the financial advisor actually calls Vicki in my Phoenix office and so she quickly sets up a 15 minute form and I talked and so Brock has asked him if I would give 15 minutes or 20 minutes whatever on the phone to this is a big fan of mine who got introduced to me by a dentist who is in the GKIC world so here's a $19,000 consulting day and the derm in his whole troop of people load their butts up and come to Cleveland and I didn't do anything directly to get any of this but because people are talking about so talked about equal sought another really good reason for it is price elasticity so in every field in everything you're in there are people doing what you do who get paid relatively little and there are people doing what you do who get paid huge amounts of money and or get paid differently and the reason is rarely a proportionate differential in core competency there's usually some differential but it is not proportionate price elasticity does not come at least solely or predominantly from a big difference in core deliverables that's why ultimately you reach a point where there's no economic gain to keep improving on your core deliverables or your core skill because as you break through into high levels of income and wealth and autonomy linked to it high price or fee elasticity all that has mostly to do with who you are perceived to be not what it is that you do so if you want a lot of fee elasticity then know that that will not come from endlessly getting better and better and better at whatever it is that you do so for at least 12 years in the in the professional speaker category other than celebrities who are paid to be celebrities so I'm throwing out Arnold Schwarzenegger as a has a comparable and I'm talking about the zigs and the brines and the Tommy's and the Dan's and the Nido cabins and the Seth Godin's and the those people of the world I was certainly per hour the highest paid of any of them I would rank me as maybe in the middle in terms of core away I stutter a little I mean my body language isn't good I'm dependent on the thing so those critiques which I never allowed written performance evaluations sometimes when you speak like for an you're the most referenced and mentioned and discussed person by everybody in the room it seems in every room now here's what I think is really important about that that's not an accident it's not organic it doesn't happen it's true by the way but it does it does it hasn't happened and it doesn't happen just because I'm really good at what I do and they have benefited from what it is that I do that's the ante to get in the poker game but that only gets me a seat at the green felt table being talked about and referenced is because I make a big point of being in front of them all the time and reinforcing and building the legend so that when a topic comes up their subconscious synapses go through their search engine and they pull me out that doesn't happen by accident that happens on purpose so I say there are we're going to leap to your page nine I say there are 16 what I call golden threads that run through and are held in common by just about everybody who achieves this legendary status in their field in their category in their marketplace in fiction has a fictional character or has a non-fictional character or is an actual living breathing real life character so for the past few weeks because Leno's leaving left now last night he's a comedy genius this is like been said a bazillion times I would suggest to you he is far from that I would suggest to you his he has been a very smart very competent very effective keeper of the keys of the tonight show he's an efficient comedian I think but comedy genius is something of an exaggeration but what exaggerates it is near death so people are always much kinder and and and you know so my legend rose each time I threatened to leave so I have you know come very close to retirement on three different occasions some years ago we had a big retirement roast the gk i c super conference and everybody came in tuxedos and we did a big roast and all that and I was supposed to go away and somewhat like Leno I didn't but but the legend rose you know everybody got more generous so anyway so here's these golden threads and I'm just going to go through the moment to spend more time with some than others one is you need an origin story all right now that may or may not be your cardboard box to you know it may predate that it may connect to that but the purpose of an origin story is to explain yourself in a way that people can understand and accept so arguably now Disney's doing their best to change this but in total over time and I had a head start too Superman and Batman are the two most valuable superhero properties in the world Neck and neck by the way in terms of total money made off of these two characters they both have very powerful and necessary origin stories so Superman's origin story is nearly identical to the Jesus Christ origin story ironically created by a couple of Jewish guys from Cleveland but here's why so Superman's origin story is can you tell me Superman's origin story in 30 seconds or less okay close yeah parents sent him from outer space planet disintegrating came here he's an alien right which the TV show Smallville probably did the best of anything contemporarily with that so here's why that origin story is vital because other than the little green rocks right he's you can't shoot him you can't stop him you can't trap him you can't well that's a terrifying thing for us mortals without the origin story Batman's even less palatable without the origin story especially the Dark Knight version of Batman which was the earliest version of Batman and now if you've seen the Dark Knight trilogy they finally got it right in the movie that so here we have a guy who dresses up in this dark winged ear you know this ain't Adam West from ABC this is this dark vigilante character who early on like the shadow carried guns and kill people by the way they wrote that out fairly early but still he runs around beating the crap out of people who he disapproves of vigilantes have never been popular heroic figures the Ku Klux Klan is a group of vigilantes their popularity is very narrow so without the saw parents gunned down in an alley by a criminal without the whole origin story he doesn't make sense to us he only makes sense to us because of it and that's why every time they present him now in a new way in a new media or to a new audience they got to retell that story and those of us who know it by heart sit there in the first movie and go oh my gosh we're gonna hear this again but everybody's got to hear it because otherwise he makes no sense so your origin story is a foundational brick to creating legend and you've got a either got to have it and now figure out how to tell it or you've got to kind of reach back in and find it but there's some origin story that explains yourself the mission story explains your actions so your origin story explains yourself your mission story explains your actions why it is that you do what it is that you do in the way that you do them so one of the most popular fictional characters of all time is Robin Hood everybody knows Robin Hood's mission story we can tell it in a single phrase which is useful right robbs from the rich gives to the poor with very low overhead bunch of guys live in the woods kill what they ate very different from Obama Robin Hood big overhead tiny little trickle gets to the poor right so our mission story explains our actions right so if I don't so I made a point of making my prizing of autonomy a big part of my legend jack included it in his introduction I don't think by the way he was working from notes I don't think he wrote that introduction he just got up here kind of loose and did it and that little gem is stuck in his head about me because I stick it in everybody's head about me otherwise I appear to be a pretty arbitrary diva like jackass all right if you don't understand for example that my business life is run by my personal life and I race horses five nights a week and therefore every day's got to end by 430 because I would rather go race a horse than be with any of you so you take second place in that equation and I organize my business to facilitate the way I want to live my life personally and that's my number one value above all else is autonomy I don't want to use that so see I would not have permitted this okay I'm not being mean I just wouldn't have I don't own a cell phone and you can't call me even on a landline without a phone appointment and sometimes it's a two to four week wait for paying clients to get their next phone appointment because I gang on my calls into one day a month and I blow it all out in one day and if you happen to decide the day before that it's already full you got to wait till next month's day I don't care if you're new client old client so all this stuff starts to look like I'm an arbitrary a-hole if you don't understand my mission story which above all else is autonomy for me and using everything I know about marketing to help you create autonomy for you now all of a sudden a lot of behavior that would not be acceptable suddenly becomes acceptable the adversity triumph story yours the worst thing about this story by the way is it is so common and so just as a little tip for you you have to find a way to make it not common and you can there is a contrarian position to all of these by the way so I have a client right now in the get rich in real estate business and almost everybody who sells people on getting rich in real estate has this story they all were washing dishes in their bathtub they all were broke they all lost their jobs and had their cars repossessed and they you know ron la grande that couldn't pay for the washer repair etc etc this guy has no such story he's a stanford grad he's an engineer he had a really nice career everything is fine he's just bored and looking for something else to do and discovered real estate and figured out how to engineer a system that works so I got to sell against this because I don't have it now there's a certain market that the washing dishes in the bathtub thing doesn't appeal to like guys like him so if I aim them in the right place but for the most part people want some adversity to triumph story so where I have it with him is his first two years being a real estate investor where he discovered everything was hype and everything was bullshit he had to re-engineer the whole process and he lost a bunch of money all right so I still got it I just don't have it up front but everybody likes this it's a reassuring encouragement because almost everybody you will be selling to has self image and self esteem issues regardless of by the way how much money they have fourth miracles so this is important Sherlock Holmes Houdini Batman's appeal is he's a superhero with no superpowers he can't actually get killed Jesus Christ everybody on our list performs miracles and there's a handful of miracles that everybody tells everybody else about that they perform so Tony you all know Tony I don't need to say the last name right it's like share influenza so Tony made his bones on the miracle of the fire walk not the first to do it it's a carnival trick that I've traced to the turn of the century maybe it was being done before that but I've got it all the way back to 1901 1902 1903 and it is a trick by the way so not the first to do it James Randy if you don't know the name you should google him would tell you he's certainly not the best to do it to ever do it but that was his miracle and everybody talked about it and everybody wanted to experience it for themselves because it's a miracle now that he can make you able to do as well Houdini made his bones going around escaping from jails letting the local police lock him in a cell handcuffed all of that and escaping that was his miracle John Edward how many of you know who John Edward is see so John Edwards is still probably a 50 million dollar a year guy you've seen him on tv John Edward helps you speak to your dead mother and hear from your dead mother or so he's got a psychic act in which he communicates with the dead and he does a thing called coal reading mostly and he's legendary in that environment um and so John has a miracle John can get you a message from Aunt Matilda right before everybody's eyes that you identify has having come from Aunt Matilda and no one could possibly know it or create it except Aunt Matilda he has a miracle legends have miracles now they don't have to be that they can be a miracle to the people that you wish to deal with so the same shtick I just described to you which is not shtick it is how I actually live see it is a miracle an enviable miracle to a lot of people that I conduct business with that I don't have a cell I don't have a pad I don't have any electronic device whatsoever I don't use email therefore I never have to check email I don't do Facebook therefore I never have to check it I never have to worry about it I've never looked at it I'm never going to look at it you can't call me you got to make an appointment see that's a miracle how can you possibly conduct business that way it is as if I just walked on fire now I won't impress everybody but it will impress people I wish to impress size of fees right one of the great things about charging either incredibly high fees or getting paid in some unique and unusual way that equates to high income so I have a client for example she has the largest expense reduction consultancy working with hospitals and they are paid 33 percent of all the overpayments recovered for the last three years and 33 percent of the documented savings going forward for the next three years right it adds up to mega mega mega money that combined with a client list like Cleveland Clinic and so forth as soon as you say it people like because of 33 percent and then when you tell them that in the last five years they've recovered six hundred million dollars worth of overpayments even people bad at math can get close six hundred million 33 percent throw out the three ten times three okay they can and they do they get there right he had a immediate physical and visual reaction to Charlie Martin's average case so that can be profound that can be a miracle literally your fees what you get paid how you get paid right I gave you an article in here we're going to skip over it but but this is about the guy who popularized I'm on you're on page 10 a long time member of mine popularized kettlebell exercising here in the United States and he's got a great mission story example this guy under miracles real quick so this is a guy by the name of jr brinkley there's two books written about him one is about him it's called charlotte and I forget the author's name and then there's a book called making them believe as well as a home study course with archive items and stuff that I did with a relative of his so brinkley was a the richest doctor in America so those of you who are in the health field and the name means nothing to you shame on you everybody else you might not know but so brinkley was a turn of century the richest doctor in America a lot of firsts he was the first guy to ever use radio beat I don't know if you know that but he's the first guy not just in health first guy period to use radio shows as a marketing vehicle the American medical association was created to put him out of business so that was a first he marketed and became rich marketing the first cure for erectile dysfunction so long before the little blue pill jr brinkley had people getting on trains coming from all across the country to his hospital he didn't call it an office he didn't call it a clinic he called it a hospital coming to his hospital to get goat testicle grafted transplants to their testicles so he took goat glands because it was very well known we were in an agricultural society then if you don't know it goats are reportedly the randiest little critters on the planet and so everybody understood goat virility and so people came and paid then thousands of dollars in 1908 uh 1915 1920 to lay on an operating table and have brinkley or one of his associate docs take some goat glands and stitch them to their testicles I assume anesthetic was not what it is today so not only was this an expensive proposition my guess is it was a painful proposition and pretty much anybody with a medical background will tell you it is pretty much a useless proposition however tens of thousands of such operations were conducted over a period of years and what drove it more than anything else is what is now referred to as the placebo effect um people got miraculous results and the pa and ma story which was his most famous story was of a guy who completely I had what we would now call chronic terminal unfixable erectile dysfunction 60 year old farmer who went home from his operation and immediately got ma pregnant and started kicking out kids like he was 20 and the pa and ma miracle was a big part of his marketing he's an interesting guy to study number five on our list is divisiveness almost every legend is divisive they divide and conquer some people feel profoundly one way about them and other people feel profoundly another way about them this seems quaint by today's standards but uh there was a whole federal government driven naturally grandstanding politician driven naturally effort to eliminate comic books uh because they were ruining the morality and the minds of america's children and the comic book industry came this close to being made illegal entirely they barely avoided it how they avoided it is beyond our time and scope today but it is instructive by the way if you want to go back and look at the history lesson but comic books and heroes like batman and superman were incredibly divisive characters in their rise to power and influence often a legend revolves around one or more very divisive acts so we're ronald reagan on the low events four times and um every once while somebody would be on there that they would keep boxed up and nobody got to hang around with but reagan was a fairly social animal although he was clearly beginning to to disintegrate at that time um but because i was last uh has jack said the good news about that is i always followed the last famous person of the day who they stuck last to keep everybody there all day long buying stuff so for one of the years colin paul was on almost the entire time ahead of me i got to know him very well schwarzkamer regan was on four times but he was on four times immediately before me all four of those times and uh i have a picture of me with reagan and so we did these photo ops at the end of the day that people paid extra to get the pictures so i have a picture of me with reagan and reagan got in a position like this and people just stepped in stepped out stepped in stepped out it looks so phony i can't use it i have a picture of me from a state fairer at a with a cardboard madonna that looks real enough to use and a picture with the real reagan looks so bad you can't even use it but anyway i said to reagan i said there's one other question i asked him is i said of everything you did in your presidency what did you do you think that created the most power for you that gave you the greatest ability to get things done and he said unquestionably firing the air traffic controllers he said that's not why i did it i said but the net result of that is internationally everybody believed i might actually be dumb enough to push the button he said domestically everybody knew that if they pushed me against a wall and i issued a threat i was going to keep it so he said from there on out nobody wants to push all the way to that kind of a result very divisive action unions went you know nuts i mean air traffic controllers if you were commercially traveling at the time you know they were picketing outside airports accosting you getting in your way as you were going to the airport for years after this happened foolishly believing that somehow they were going to get this reversed very divisive action it's useful to pick on somebody that you want to demonize by the way anytime a leader gets in trouble that's exactly what they do all over again right is they pick somebody who's not going to buy from them not going to support them not going to vote for them and they target them they create a new kind of divisiveness so when you go on the attack and you think about these legends you have you have so let's see so christ who's who's the enemy well literally yeah i mean literally the established order right i mean this guy was anti-establishment and it got a rather profound uh to be a revolution so divisiveness uh i read it on your own there's an article i gave you that starts on page 13 it describes a very interesting and very divisive guy who broke into a niche market in exactly the same way i broke into the speaker market as a marketing expert many many moons ago and and i was so divisive there are still people in the national speakers association who detest me many of them have died but um well because they were at least a zigz age they're like 15 years older than i am so you know your enemies you know friends come and go enemies accumulate but if you live long enough a lot of them die um but the ones that are still around there are people who will not speak to me if i'm coming through a hallway they go to the other side of the hotel in order to walk and not come into contact with me and that's 30 some odd years ago i have trouble remembering who they are to know that they're aggravated at me and they are still and i mean i'm irish so you know the um irish alzheimer's as you forget everything but your grudges so i mean i i like get it but still it's remarkable to me but that's how divisive i was and it was immensely profitable to be so um so next can be succinctly summarized get rid of the carbs everything else will take care of itself pretty simple pretty clear at the time pretty controversial iron rand began as a fiction writer actually just
